name: Orbital Mechanics Toolkit
summary: A practical decision framework for orbital manoeuvre planning. Given a starting orbit and a target orbit, systematically evaluate transfer options, timing constraints, and propellant costs to find the optimal trajectory.
whenToUseIt: Use this toolkit whenever planning orbital transfers, constellation deployment, rendezvous operations, or deorbit manoeuvres. It structures the decision-making process from mission constraints to specific burn plans.
steps: Calculate Hohmann transfer delta-v as the baseline
Evaluate alternatives: bi-elliptic transfer, low-thrust spiral, gravity assist
Factor in timing constraints — launch windows, phasing orbits, synodic periods
Account for plane change costs (inclination changes are expensive)
Select the transfer method that minimises total delta-v within timing constraints
Plan the burn sequence — ignition time, duration, attitude
example: Deploying a Starlink satellite from the parking orbit (300 km, 53 deg inclination) to its operational orbit (550 km, 53 deg). Hohmann transfer delta-v: about 140 m/s. But Starlink uses electric propulsion (Hall thrusters, Isp ~1,500s), so it does a slow spiral transfer over several weeks instead. The low thrust means higher total delta-v (~200 m/s) but vastly less propellant mass because of the high Isp. The trade-off: time vs mass.
